expectations.text: Attacks the strongest claims first (decision compression, demo-as-spec), not peripheral weaknesses
expectations.evidence: Lead challenge targets 'decision compression' directly — argues it's really 'decision front-loading' and the comparison baseline is unfair. Second challenge hits sample size.

expectations.text: Provides the strongest version of the opposing view — not a straw man but a real argument someone would make
expectations.evidence: Each counter-argument is fully articulated. E.g., challenge 3 argues a PM who thinks like this would produce similar output without Claude Code. Challenge 5 argues PRDs aren't inherently theatrical.

expectations.text: Challenges survivorship bias — only two projects, both presented as successes
expectations.evidence: Challenge 2 directly addresses two-project sample size: 'two data points drawn from the same person, at the same company, in the same quarter... is not a pattern — it's a coincidence waiting to be tested.'

expectations.text: Questions whether outcomes should be attributed to Claude Code or to the author's effort/context
expectations.evidence: Challenge 3 explicitly addresses attribution: 'The real differentiator wasn't Claude Code. It was that you voluntarily spent the time...'

expectations.text: Classifies each challenge as fatal flaw, weakness to acknowledge, or valid tradeoff
expectations.evidence: All 7 challenges have explicit verdict classifications. Most are 'weakness to acknowledge', one is 'bordering on fatal flaw if left unaddressed', one is 'valid tradeoff'.

expectations.text: Ends with 'The lesson survives if...' conditions
expectations.evidence: Clear 'The lesson survives if...' section with 3 conditions: judgment tested not just applied, comparison is fair, tool vs person question answered.
